title: Chapter 6: The False Prophet of the Silicon Temple
content: <!-- Speed: Sprint | D10+D2 -->
Los Angeles announced itself with traffic.

Not metaphorical traffic — actual gridlock, the kind where you can read an entire Wikipedia article on your phone between lane changes. Shadowfax's engine wheezed in protest. The air conditioning had been dead since Bakersfield and now, at 2 PM in the San Fernando Valley, the car was essentially a slow-cooker.
"The Infernal Gateway," Chloe announced, sweating regally. "The first trial of Avalon. Those who cannot endure the heat shall never enter the kingdom."
"It's traffic," Sam said. Her shirt was stuck to the seat. "On the 405."
"Same thing."
Sam's phone buzzed. She glanced at it, then glanced again.
"Oh no."
"What?"
"The strawberry farm video. It has 4.7 million views."
Chloe sat up straighter. "The prophecy unfolds."
"The comments are... divided. Half of them think you're a genius. Half of them think you need to be institutionalized."
"A true visionary always splits the audience."
"One comment says 'this is the most unhinged thing I've ever seen and I would die for her.'"
"That's my target demographic."

The billboard appeared at exit 27B: a man in white linen, glowing with the kind of soft-focus radiance that required a six-figure lighting rig. His arms were spread. His smile was beatific. The text read:
**DR. ZENITH MALLOCH — AWAKEN YOUR INNER GOD**
With a Venice Beach address and a phone number that spelled out 1-800-BE-DIVINE.
Chloe leaned so far forward her crown bumped the windshield. "A dark wizard."
"It's a wellness guru."
"Dark wizard. Look at his eyes — the iris dilation. He's been consuming souls."
"He's been consuming Botox."
"The Botox is a mask. Beneath it: a void where a soul should be. Classic dark wizard presentation."
Sam zoomed in on the billboard photo. "His website says he charges $5,000 for a weekend retreat. 'Discover your quantum self through breathwork and primal sound healing.'"
"That's not breathwork. That's a soul-extraction ritual disguised as breathwork. I've read about this in the ancient texts."
"What ancient texts?"
"Reddit," Chloe said. "r/WellnessScams."

Venice Beach was exactly what you'd expect: skateboards, muscle beach, someone selling crystals next to someone selling organic kale smoothies next to someone selling a $300 hoodie that looked like it had been attacked by scissors. The Temple of Zenith Malloch occupied a converted warehouse on Abbot Kinney with floor-to-ceiling windows, white everything, and a sign that said "THE FUTURE OF HUMAN CONSCIOUSNESS" in lowercase helvetica.
"We're not going in there," Sam said.
"The Queen must confront false prophets."
"The Queen's Royal Treasurer is on a budget. This place charges $18 for a smoothie."
"Then we confront them outside. Ambush strategy. Classic D&D rogue approach."

They found him outside — Zenith Malloch himself, holding court at a pop-up canopy where he was signing copies of his book *Breathe Your Way to Billionaire Consciousness.* He wore white linen pants, a white linen shirt, and white linen sandals. He looked like a marshmallow that had achieved sentience.
A small crowd had gathered — mostly people in their twenties and thirties who looked like they had money and problems, in that order.
"And when you align your chakras with your revenue streams," Zenith was saying, "the universe literally rewires itself to deliver abundance directly to your—"
"FALSE!"
Everyone turned. Chloe stood at the edge of the canopy, sword raised.
"Chakras do not align with revenue streams. Chakras are spiritual energy centers. Money is a social construct. You're performing category fraud."
Zenith smiled his beatific smile. The smile of someone who had been heckled before and had a protocol. "Ah, a skeptic. Welcome, welcome. What's your name?"
"Queen Artemis of Avalon."
"Beautiful. Beautiful energy. And what brings you to our gathering?"
"I'm here to exorcise you."
The smile flickered. Just slightly. "Exorcise?"
"You're a dark wizard masquerading as a wellness guru. You sell false enlightenment to vulnerable people at five thousand dollars a weekend. You're not a healer. You're a spiritual vampire."
A woman in the crowd — Lululemon, holding a copy of *Breathe Your Way to Billionaire Consciousness* — looked at her book. Then at Zenith. Then at Chloe.
"She has a point about the pricing," the woman said.
"The price reflects the value of transformation," Zenith said smoothly. "True awakening isn't cheap."
"True awakening is free," Chloe said. "Grass is free. Breathing is free. Calling your mother is free. You're charging people for things that belong to them."
Zenith's smile had developed a crack. "I've dedicated my life to helping people—"

"No. You've dedicated your life to monetizing their loneliness. There's a difference. A real healer would be at a free clinic. You're at a pop-up canopy charging for signed copies of a book where the main advice is 'breathe and be rich.' That's not healing. That's grift."
The crowd was shifting. A man put down his copy of the book. The Lululemon woman opened her Notes app and started typing.
"If I may—" Zenith began.

"You may not," Chloe said. "You've been talking for years. It's someone else's turn." She turned to the crowd. "This man's real name is Kevin. I know because I Googled him while Sam was parking. His diploma is from an unaccredited online school. His 'ancient breathing technique' is just the Wim Hof Method with different branding. And he has three Yelp reviews that mention bedbugs at his retreat center."
Zenith's beatific expression had fully collapsed. Beneath it was a man who had been selling enlightenment for a decade and had never expected a foam-sword audit.
"That's... defamation."
"It's not defamation if it's true," Sam said, holding up her phone. "I have screenshots."
Zenith looked at Sam. Then at Chloe. Then at his dwindling crowd. "Security!"
A man in a white t-shirt that said "AWAKENED" approached. He was large but had the energy of someone who had been hired for his aura, not his competence.
"Sir, do you want me to—"
"I don't know, Kevin," Chloe said to the security guard. "Do you?"
The security guard stopped. "My name's not Kevin."
"Your boss's name is Kevin. The guy who pays you to guard his enlightenment scam. How do you feel about that?"

The security guard looked at Zenith. Zenith looked at the security guard. Something passed between them — something that suggested the security guard had not, in fact, been paid on time for the last two months.
"You know what," the security guard said, "I'm going to take my break."
He walked away. The crowd walked away. Eventually, Zenith Malloch stood alone under his pop-up canopy, holding unsigned copies of a book no one wanted anymore.
Back in the car, Sam updated the spreadsheet.

| Enemy | Type | Status |
|-------|------|--------|
| Dave from Gas Station | Lesser Troll | Exorcised (possibly called his own mother) |
| Strawberry Farm Influencers | Vanity Demons | Liberated (one reconciliating with mom) |
| Zenith "Kevin" Malloch | False Prophet / Dark Wizard | Publicly Audited |
"Three for three," Sam said.
"The quest proceeds according to prophecy."
"What prophecy?"
"The one I'm making up as we go."
Sam stared at her. "You're improvising the entire mythology."
"All mythologies are improvised. The only difference between me and the Greeks is that they had better PR."

The sun was setting over Santa Monica. The Pacific was doing that thing where it looked like a sheet of gold. Chloe watched it through the window, crown tilted, poncho still somehow majestic despite the sweat stains.
"We're almost there," she said quietly. "Avalon."
"LA."
"Same coordinates."
Sam turned the key. Shadowfax coughed, sputtered, and on the fourth try, started. The playlist shuffled to Hozier. And they drove into the golden light.
